title: IELTS Reading — Cambridge 10 Test 3
bandHint: IELTS Academic · Cambridge 10 · Test 3 · 3 passages · 40 câu

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 1–13.
parts.passageTitle: The context, meaning and scope of tourism

passage.label: A
passage.text: Travel has existed since the beginning of time, when primitive man set out, often traversing great distances in search of game, which provided the food and clothing necessary for his survival. Throughout the course of history, people have travelled for purposes of trade , religiou s conviction, economic gain. war, migration and other equally compe lling motivations. Jn the Roman era, wealthy aristocrats and high government officials also travelled for pleasure. Seaside resorts located at Pompeii and Herculaneum afforded citize ns the opportunity to escape to their vacation villas in order to avoid the summer heat of Rome . Travel, excep t during the Dark Ages, has continued to grow and, throughout recorded history, has played a vital role in the development of civilisations and their economies .

passage.label: B
passage.text: Tourism in the mass form as we know it today is a distinctly twentieth-century phenomenon. Historians suggest that the advent of mass tourism began in England during the industrial revolution with the rise of the middle class and the availability of relatively inexpensive transportation. The creation of the comme rcial airline industry following the Second World War and the subsequent development of the jet aircraft in the 1950s signalled the rapid growth and expansion of international travel. This growth led to the development of a major new industry: tourism. In turn, international tourism became the concern of a number of world governments since it not only provided new employment opportunities but also produced a means of earning foreign exchange.

passage.label: C
passage.text: Tourism today has grown significantly in both economic and social importance . In most industrialised countries over the past few years the fastest growth has been seen in the area of services. One of the largest segments of the service industry, although largely unrecognised as an entity in some of these countries, is travel and tourism. According to the World Travel and Tourism Council (1992), 'Travel and tourism is the largest industry in the world on virtually any economic meas ure including value-added capital investment, employment and tax contribution s'. In 1992, the industry's gross output was estimated to be $3.5 trillion, over 12 per cent of all consumer spending . The travel and tourism industry is the world's largest employer with almost 130 million jobs, or almost 7 per cent of all employee s. Thi s industry is the world's leading industrial contributor, producing over 6 per cent of the world's gross national product and accounting for capital investment in excess of $422 billion in direct, indirect and personal taxes each year. Thu s, tour ism has a profound impact both on the world economy and, because of the educative effect of travel and the effects on employment , on society itself. Test 3

passage.label: D
passage.text: However, the major problems of the travel and tourism industry that have hidden, or obscured, its economic impact are the diversity and fragmentation of the industry itself. The travel industry includes: hotels , motels and other types of accommodation; restaurants and other food services; transportat ion services and facilities; amusements, attractions and other leisure facilities; gift shops and a large number of other enterprises. Since many of these businesses also serve local residents, the impact of spending by visitors can easily be overlooked or underestimated. In addition, Meis (1992) points out that the tourism industry involves concepts that have remained amorphou s to both analysts and decision makers. Moreover, in all nations this problem has made it difficult for the industry to develop any type of reliable or credible tourism information base in order to estimate the contribution it makes to regionalt national and global economies. However, the nature of this very diversity makes travel and tourism ideaJ vehicles for economic development in a wide variety of countries, regions or communities.

passage.label: E
passage.text: Once the exclusive province of the wealthy, travel and tourism have become an institutionali sed way of life for most of the population. In fact, Mcintosh and Goeldner ( 1990) suggest that tourism has become the largest commodity in international trade for many nations and, for a significant number of other countries, it ranks second or third. For example, tourism is the major source of income in Bermuda , Greece, Italy, Spain, Switzerland and most Caribbean count ries. In addition, Hawkins and Ritchie , quoting from data published by the American Express Company, suggest that the travel and tourism industry is the number one ranked employer in the Bahama s, Brazil , Canada, France, (the former) West Germany, Hong Kong, Italy, Jamaica, Japan, Singapore, the United Kingdom and the United States. However, because of problem s of definition, which directly affect statistical measurement, it is not possible with any degree of certainty to provide precise, valid or reliable data about the extent of world-wide tourism participation or its economic impact. In many cases, similar difficulties arise when attempts are made to measure domestic tourism. 66 Reading Questions ~ 10 Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1? In boxes 5-10 on your answer sheet, write TRUE FALSE NOT GIVEN if the statement agrees with the information if the statement contradicts the information if there is no information on this 5 The largest employment figures in the world are found in the travel and tourism industry. 6 Tourism contributes over six per cent of the Australian gross national product. 7 Tourism has a social impact because it promotes recreation . 8 Two main features of the travel and tourism industry make its economic significance difficult to ascertain. 9 Visitor spending is always greater than the spending of residents in tourist areas . 1 O It is easy to show statistically how tourism affects individual economies .

questionGroups.instruction: Reading Passage 1 has five paragraphs, A–E. Choose the correct heading for paragraphs B–E.

headings.label: Economic and social significance of tourism
Difficulty in recognising the economic effects of tourism
The contribution of air travel to tourism

questions.prompt: Paragraph B
questions.explanation: Đoạn B: du lịch đại chúng thế kỷ 20.

questions.prompt: Paragraph C
questions.explanation: Đoạn C: tầm quan trọng kinh tế-xã hội.

questions.prompt: Paragraph D
questions.explanation: Đoạn D: khó đo lường tác động kinh tế.

questions.prompt: Paragraph E
questions.explanation: Đoạn E: tác động toàn cầu, khó thống kê chính xác.

questionGroups.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 1? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.

questions.prompt: The largest employment figures in the world are found in the travel and tourism industry.
questions.explanation: Ngành employer lớn nhất thế giới ~130 triệu việc làm.

questions.prompt: Tourism contributes over six per cent of the Australian gross national product.
questions.explanation: 6% GNP thế giới — không nói Australia.

questions.prompt: Tourism has a social impact because it promotes recreation.
questions.explanation: Tác động xã hội qua giáo dục và việc làm — không nhắc recreation.

questions.prompt: Two main features of the travel and tourism industry make its economic significance difficult to ascertain.
questions.explanation: Đa dạng, phân mảnh và khái niệm mơ hồ.

questions.prompt: Visitor spending is always greater than the spending of residents in tourist areas.
questions.explanation: Chi tiêu du khách dễ bị bỏ sót — không nói luôn lớn hơn.

questions.prompt: It is easy to show statistically how tourism affects individual economies.
questions.explanation: Không thể cung cấp dữ liệu chính xác, đáng tin.

questionGroups.instruction: Complete the sentences below. Choose NO MORE THAN THREE WORDS from the passage for each answer.

questions.prompt: In Greece, tourism is the most important ___.
questions.explanation: Nguồn thu chính ở Hy Lạp.

questions.prompt: The travel and tourism industry in Jamaica is the major ___.
questions.explanation: Nhà tuyển dụng lớn nhất Jamaica.

questions.prompt: Problems measuring international tourism are often reflected in the measurement of ___.
questions.explanation: Khó đo cả du lịch nội địa.

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 14–26.
parts.passageSubtitle: Canadian writer Jay Ingram investigates the mystery of why leaves turn red in the fall.

passage.text: Autw•mn leaves Canadian writer Jay Ingram investigates the mystery of why leaves turn red in the fall A One of the most captivating natural events of the year in many areas throughout North America is the turning of the leaves in the fall. The colours are magnificent, but the questio n of exac tly why some trees tum yellow or orange, and others red or purple , is something which has long puzzled scientists .

passage.label: B
passage.text: Summer leaves are green because they are full of chlorophyll , the molecule that captures sunlight and converts that energy into new building material s for the tree. As fall appro aches in the northern hemisphere , the amount of solar energy availab le declines considerably. For many trees - evergreen conifers being an exception - the best strategy is to abandon photosynthesis• until the spring. So rather than maintaining the now redundant leaves throughout the winter, the tree saves it.., precious resources and discards them. But befor e letting its leaves go, the tree dismantles their chlorophyll molecules and ships their valuable nitrogen back into the twigs. As chlorophy ll is depleted, other colours that have been dominated by it throughout the summe r begin to be revealed. This unmasking explains the autumn colours of yellow and orange , but not the brilliant reds and purples of trees such as the maple or sumac.

passage.label: C
passage.text: The source of the red is widely known: it is created by anthocyanins , water-solub le plant pigments reflecting the red to blue range of the visible spectrum . They belong to a class of sugar-based chemical compo unds also known as ftavonoids. What's puzzling is that anth ocyanins are actually newly minted , made in the leaves at the same time as the tree is preparing to drop them . But it is hard to make sense of the manufac ture of anthocyani.ns - why should a tre e bother making new chemic als in its leaves when it's already scrambling to withdraw and preseive the ones already there'?

passage.label: D
passage.text: Some theories about anthocyani.ns have argued that they might act as a chemical defence against atta cks by insects or fungi, or that they might attract fruit-eating birds or increase a leafs tole rance to freezing. However there are problems with each of these theories, including the fact that leaves are red for such a relatively short period that the expe nse of energy needed to manuf actu re the anthocyanins would outweigh any anti-fungal or anti-herbivo re activity achieved . •photosynthesis: the production of new material from sunlight, water and carbon dioxide 68 Reading

passage.label: E
passage.text: It has also been proposed that trees may produce vivid red colours to convince herbivorous insects that they are healthy and robust and would be easily able to mount chemical defences against infestation. If insects paid attention to such advertisements , they might be prompted to lay their eggs on a duller, and presumably less resistant host The flaw in this theory lies in the lack of proof to support it. No one has as yet ascertained whether more robust trees sport the brightest leaves, or whether insects make choices according to colour intensity.

passage.label: F
passage.text: Perhaps the most plausible suggestion as to why leaves would go to the trouble of making antho cyanins when they're busy packing up for the winter is the theory known as the 'light screen ' hypothesis . It sounds paradoxical , because the idea behind this hypothesis is that the red pigment is made in autumn leaves to protect chlorophyll, the light-absorbing chemical , from too much light. Why does chlorophyll need protection when it is the natural world's supreme light absorber ? Why prote ct chlorophy ll at a time when the tree is brealdng it down to salvage as much of it as possible?

passage.label: G
passage.text: Chlorophyll , although exquisitely evolved to capture the energy of sunlight, can sometimes be overwhelmed by it, especially in situations of drought , low temperature s, or nutrient deficiency. Moreover , the problem of oversensitivity to light is even more acute in the fall, when the leaf is busy preparing for winter by dismantling its internal machinery. The energy absorbed by the chlo rophyll molecules of the unstable autumn leaf is not immediately channelled into useful produ cts and processes , as it would be in an intact summer leaf. The weakened fall leaf then becomes vulnerable to the highly destructive effects of the oxygen creat ed by the excited chlorophyll molecules.

passage.label: H
passage.text: Even if you had never suspected that this is what was going on when leaves tum red, there are clues out there. One is straightforward : on many trees , the leaves that are the reddest are those on the side of the tree which gets most sun. Not only that, but the red is brighter on the upper side of the leaf. It has also been recognised for decades that the best conditions for intense red colours are dry, sunny days and cool nights , conditions that nicely match those that make leaves susceptible to excess light. And finally , trees such as maples usually get much redder the more north you travel in the northern hemisphere. It's colder ther e, they're more stres sed, their chlorophyll is more sens itive and it needs more SW\block. What is still not fully understood , however , is why some trees resort to producing red pigments while others don't bother, and simp ly reveal their orange or yellow hues. Do these trees have othe r means at their disposal to prevent overexposure to light in autumn? Their story , though not as spectacular to the eye, will surely turn out to be as subtle and as complex. Test 3

questionGroups.instruction: Reading Passage 2 has nine paragraphs, A–I. Which paragraph contains the following information?
questionGroups.note: NB You may use any letter more than once.

questions.prompt: a description of the substance responsible for the red colouration of leaves
questions.explanation: Anthocyanins tạo màu đỏ.

questions.prompt: the reason why trees drop their leaves in autumn
questions.explanation: Bỏ lá khi giảm quang hợp — tiết kiệm nitrogen.

questions.prompt: some evidence to confirm a theory about the purpose of the red leaves
questions.explanation: Lá đỏ nhất hướng nhiều nắng — bằng chứng light screen.

questions.prompt: an explanation of the function of chlorophyll
questions.explanation: Đoạn E thảo luận cây khỏe mạnh — chlorophyll được nhắc ở B/G; đáp án E theo key.

questions.prompt: a suggestion that the red colouration in leaves could serve as a warning signal
questions.explanation: Cây khỏe mạnh để côn trùng không đẻ trứng.

questionGroups.instruction: Complete the notes below. Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.

questions.prompt: The most vividly coloured red leaves are found on the side of the tree facing the ___.
questions.explanation: Mặt hướng nhiều nắng nhất.

questions.prompt: The ___ surfaces of leaves contain the most red pigment.
questions.explanation: Mặt trên lá đỏ hơn.

questions.prompt: Red leaves are most abundant when daytime weather conditions are ___ and sunny.
questions.explanation: Ngày khô, nắng, đêm mát.

questions.prompt: The intensity of the red colour of leaves increases as you go further ___.
questions.explanation: Càng về Bắc càng đỏ.

questionGroups.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the information given in Reading Passage 2? Write TRUE, FALSE or NOT GIVEN.

questions.prompt: It is likely that the red pigments help to protect the leaf from freezing temperatures.
questions.explanation: Lý thuyết chống đông bị bác — thời gian đỏ quá ngắn.

questions.prompt: The 'light screen' hypothesis would initially seem to contradict what is known about chlorophyll.
questions.explanation: Nghe nghịch lý — bảo vệ chất đang bị tháo dỡ.

questions.prompt: Leaves which turn colours other than red are more likely to be damaged by sunlight.
questions.explanation: Cây khác không làm anthocyanins — không nói hư hại nhiều hơn.

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.

questions.prompt: For which of the following questions does the writer offer an explanation?

options.label: why conifers remain green in winter
how leaves turn orange and yellow in autumn
how herbivorous insects choose which trees to lay their eggs in
why anthocyanins are restricted to certain trees

questions.explanation: Giải thích vàng/cam khi chlorophyll mất — anthocyanins không giải thích được.

parts.rangeLabel: Read the text and answer questions 27–40.
parts.passageSubtitle: Ancient voyagers who settled the far-flung islands of the Pacific Ocean.

passage.text: Beyond the blue horizon Ancient voyagers who settled the far-flung islands of the Pacific Ocean An important archaeological discovery on the island of ~fare in che Pacific archipelago of Vanuacu has revealed craces of an ancient seafaring people, che distant ancescors of coday's Polynesians. The sire came co light only by chance.

An agricultural worker, digging in che grounds of a derelict plantation, scraped open a grave - che first of dozens in a burial ground some 3,000 years old. It is che oldest cemetery ever found in che Pacific islands, and ic harbors che remains of an :rncienc peopl~ :m:h:ieologins call che Lapica.
They were daring blue-water adventu rers who used basic canoes co rove across the ocean.

Bue they were not just explorers. They were also pioneers who carried with chem everything chey would need co build new lives - their livestock, taro seedlings and scone tools. Within the span of several centuries, che Lapita screeched rhe boundaries of their world from che jungle-dad volcanoes of Papua New Guinea to chc loneliest coral oucliers of Tonga.

The Lapita lefr precious few clues abouc themselves, but Eface expands the volume of data available co researchers dramatically. The remains of 62 individuals have been uncovered so far, and archacologiscs were also thrilled to find six complete Lapita pots. Other iccms included a l.apita burial urn with modeled birds arranged on che rim as though peering down at the human remains scaled inside. 'lc's an important discovery,' says Manhew Spriggs, professor of archaeology at the Auscralian National Universiry and head of 72 the international ream digging up the site, 'for ic conclusively identifies che remains as Lapica.' DNA ceased from these human remains may help answer one of che most puzzling questions in Pacific anthropology: did all Pacific islanders spring from one source or many? Was there onJy one oucward migration from a single point in Asia, or several from different poincs? 'This reprcsencs the best opporrunjry we've had yet,' says Spriggs, 'co find our who the Lapica actually were, where chcy came from, and who cheir closest descendants are coday.' There is one st ubborn question for which archaeology has yet to provide any answers: how did the l.apita accomplish che ancient equivalent of a moon landing, many times over? No-one has found one of their canoes or any rigging, which could reveal how the canoes were sailed.

Nor do che oral hiscorics and rradirions of later Polynesians offer any insighcs, for rhey mm inco myths long before they reach as far back in rime as che Lapita. 'All we can say for certain is that the l..apita had canoes that were capable of ocean voyages, and they had the ability co sail chem,' says Geoff lrwin, a professor of archaeology ar the Univmity of Auckland. Those sailing skills, he says, were developed and ~ down over thousands of years by earlier mariners who worked thcir way through the archipelagocs of che western Pacific, making shorr crossings to nearby islands. The real advenrure didn't begin, however, uncil cheir l..apica descendants sailed out of sight of land, wich empty horizons on every side. This muse have been as difficult for chem as landing on the moon is for us coday. Certainly it distinguished them from their ancestors, but what gave chem the courage to launch out on such risky voyages? Reading easy. Vanuaru, for example, stretches more than 500 miles in a norchwesc-southeasc crend, ics scores of incervisible islands forming a backstop for mariners riding the trade winds home.

AJJ chis presupposes one essential detail, says Atholl Anderson, professor of prehistory at the Australian National University: che Lapita had mastered the advanced art of sailing against che wind. J\nd there's no proof chey could do any such thing,' Anderson says. 'There has been this assumption they did, and people have: built ca.noes to re-create: those early voyages based on chat assumption. Bur nobody has any idea what their canoes looked like or how they were rigged.' Rather than give all the credit ro human The Lapita's th ruse into the Pacific was skill, Anderson invokes the winds of chance. El eastward, against rhc: prevailing crade winds, Nmo, the same climate disruption that affects the Irwin notes. Those nagging headwinds, he argues, Pacific today, may have helped scatter the Lapira, may have been che key co their success. 'They Anderson suggests. He poincs our chat dimace dara could sail out for days into the unknown and assess obtained from slow-growing corals around the che area, secure: in the knowledge that if chc:y Pacific indicate a series of unusually frequenc El didn't find anything, rhey could turn about Ninos around the time of the Lapita expansion. By and catch a swift ride back on the trade winds. reversing the regular easr-co-wesr Row of the crade This is whar would have made che whole thing winds for weeks ac a time, these 'super El Ninos' work.' Once out there, skilled seafarers would might have ca.ken the Lapica on long unplanned have detected abundant leads co follow co land: voyages. seabirds, coconucs and twigs carried our co sea by H .L d.d . h . th ·d d h c. . owever u1cy 1 1c c e Lap1ca spread e ti cs, an r e anernoon pile-up of clouds on themsdvcs th· rd f ch ' · the horizon which often indicates an island in the th called~ I . ol' e way across the Pao fie, distance. en It quits ror reasons known only co chem. Ahead lay the vase emptiness of the ccnrral Pacific and perhaps they were coo thinly screeched ro venture farther. They probably never numbered more than a few thousand in total, and in cheir rapid migration eascward they encountered hund reds of islands - more rhan 300 in Fiji alone.

For returning explorers. successful or not, the geography of rheir own archipelagoes would have provided a safety net. Without chis to go by, overshooting rheir home ports, getting lost and sailing off into eternity would have been all coo AFARINESH Test3

questionGroups.instruction: Complete the summary using the list of words and phrases, A–J, below.

questions.prompt: A 3,000-year-old burial ground … found on an abandoned ___ on Efate.
questions.explanation: Plantation bỏ hoang.

questions.prompt: The cemetery, which is a significant ___, was uncovered accidentally.
questions.explanation: Phát hiện khảo cổ quan trọng.

questions.prompt: They took many things with them including ___ and tools.
questions.explanation: Gia súc và hạt giống.

questions.prompt: Spriggs believes the ___ found at the site is very important.
questions.explanation: Burial urn xác nhận di hài Lapita.

questions.prompt: It confirms that the ___ found inside are Lapita.
questions.explanation: Xương (bones) trong urn.

questionGroups.instruction: Choose the correct letter, A, B, C or D.

questions.prompt: According to the writer, there are difficulties explaining how the Lapita accomplished their journeys because

options.label: the canoes that have been discovered offer relatively few clues.
archaeologists have shown limited interest in this area of research.
little information relating to this period can be relied upon for accuracy.
technological advances have altered the way such achievements are viewed.

questions.explanation: Không tìm thấy thuyền, rigging; truyền thuyết đã thành thần thoại.

questions.prompt: According to the sixth paragraph, what was extraordinary about the Lapita?

options.label: They sailed beyond the point where land was visible.
Their cultural heritage discouraged the expression of fear.
They were able to build canoes that withstood ocean voyages.
Their navigational skills were passed on from one generation to the next.

questions.explanation: Ra khơi khi không còn nhìn thấy đất — như đổ bộ lên Mặt Trăng.

questions.prompt: What does 'This' refer to in the seventh paragraph?

options.label: the Lapita's ability to detect signs of land
the Lapita's extensive knowledge of the region
the Lapita's belief they would be able to return home

questions.explanation: Gió ngược giúp quay về nhà trên trade winds.

questions.prompt: According to the eighth paragraph, how was the geography of the region significant?

options.label: It played an important role in Lapita culture.
It meant there were relatively few storms at sea.
It provided a navigational aid for the Lapita.
It made a large number of islands habitable.

questions.explanation: Quần đảo dài tạo 'backstop' cho hải trình về nhà.

questionGroups.instruction: Do the following statements agree with the views of the writer in Reading Passage 3? Write YES, NO or NOT GIVEN.

questions.prompt: It is now clear that the Lapita could sail into a prevailing wind.
questions.explanation: Anderson: không có bằng chứng họ lướt ngược gió.

questions.prompt: Extreme climate conditions may have played a role in Lapita migration.
questions.explanation: El Niño có thể đẩy họ đi xa không chủ đích.

questions.prompt: The Lapita learnt to predict the duration of El Niños.
questions.explanation: El Niño được nhắc nhưng không nói dự báo.

questions.prompt: It remains unclear why the Lapita halted their expansion across the Pacific.
questions.explanation: Dừng ở Fiji — lý do không rõ.

questions.prompt: It is likely that the majority of Lapita settled on Fiji.
questions.explanation: Gặp hàng trăm đảo kể cả Fiji — không nói đa số định cư ở Fiji.
